title_en: Divine Acts and the Question of Creation
content_en:
Among the most important and technically demanding sections of Al-Irshad is its treatment of divine action and creation. This includes questions about the nature of God's relationship to the created world, the reality of causation, the obligations of God toward His creation (if any), and the question of whether God's actions can be evaluated in terms of justice or wisdom.

Al-Juwayni's treatment of causation reflects the Ash'ari occasionalist position, which he develops and defends with precision. The Ash'ari school held that apparent causal connections between natural events — fire burning, food nourishing, medicine healing — are not real causal necessities but are God's customary pattern of creating effects in conjunction with causes. God alone is the real agent of all effects; created things have no intrinsic causal power. Fire does not burn by its own nature; God creates the burning in conjunction with the presence of fire, in accordance with His established pattern (adah).

This position had important theological motivations. If created things had genuine independent causal power, then God would be constrained by a natural order He had not directly created and could not immediately alter. Miracles — central to the proof of prophethood — require that God can suspend the ordinary pattern of events. If natural regularities were genuinely necessary causal connections, miracles would be impossible. The Ash'ari occasionalist framework makes miracles conceptually straightforward: God simply deviates from His customary pattern of conjoined creation.

Al-Juwayni also addresses the question of divine obligation — the Mu'tazili doctrine that divine justice requires God to do what is best for His creatures and to refrain from what would be unjust. The Mu'tazila argued that God is obligated by rational necessity to reward the obedient and punish the wicked, to send prophets, and to provide the rational and revelatory guidance humans need to fulfill their obligations. Against this, al-Juwayni defends the Ash'ari position that God has no obligations — He is the absolute sovereign over His creation and can do whatever He wills with it. The concepts of justice and injustice that apply to created agents do not constrain God, who is subject to no external standard.

This position might seem to undermine the basis for trusting divine promises and prophecy, but al-Juwayni argues that it does not: while God has no obligation to fulfill His promises, His truthfulness means that He will do so. The guarantee of divine reliability comes not from a rational obligation derived from the concept of justice but from the divine attribute of truthfulness, which cannot be negated.

These positions on causation and divine obligation distinguish the Ash'ari school sharply from both the philosophers and the Mu'tazila and represent some of its most distinctive and discussed contributions to Islamic theology.
content_id:
Al-Irshad membahas salah satu perdebatan terpanas dalam teologi kalam: apakah Allah wajib melakukan yang terbaik bagi hamba-Nya (al-aslah)? Dan apakah ada hubungan sebab-akibat yang nyata antara peristiwa-peristiwa alam?

Kaum Mu'tazilah berpendapat bahwa Allah wajib melakukan yang terbaik untuk makhluk-Nya, dan bahwa Allah tidak dapat berbuat zalim karena hal itu bertentangan dengan keadilan-Nya. Mereka juga menerima kausalitas alam sebagai kenyataan yang nyata — api membakar karena sifatnya sebagai api.

Tradisi Asy'ariyyah, yang diikuti al-Juwayni, menolak konsep kewajiban bagi Allah. Allah tidak diwajibkan oleh siapapun untuk melakukan apapun — baik keadilan, kebaikan, maupun hal terbaik. Segala sesuatu yang Allah lakukan adalah atas kehendak mutlak-Nya. Menetapkan kewajiban bagi Allah, menurut mereka, berarti membatasi kekuasaan Allah dan menempatkan sesuatu di atas-Nya.

Dalam persoalan kausalitas, al-Juwayni mengikuti posisi Asy'ariyyah yang terkenal dengan doktrin okasionalisme (al-'adat): apa yang kita sebut sebab-akibat sebenarnya bukanlah hubungan yang niscaya, melainkan kebiasaan yang Allah ciptakan secara terus-menerus. Api tidak memiliki daya untuk membakar dengan sendirinya; Allah-lah yang menciptakan pembakaran bersamaan dengan kontak api dan benda yang terbakar. Ini berbeda dari filsafat Aristotelian yang menetapkan kausalitas sebagai sifat inheren benda-benda alam.

Perdebatan ini memiliki implikasi langsung pada persoalan mukjizat: jika kausalitas tidak niscaya tetapi hanya kebiasaan, maka mukjizat adalah ketika Allah memilih untuk tidak mengikuti kebiasaan tersebut — bukan pelanggaran terhadap hukum alam yang tetap.

Al-Juwayni juga membahas persoalan taklif (pembebanan kewajiban): apakah Allah wajib memberikan imbalan bagi ketaatan dan hukuman bagi kemaksiatan? Tradisi Asy'ariyyah menjawab: tidak ada kewajiban bagi Allah, tetapi Allah memang menjanjikan hal itu dalam wahyu-Nya dan janji Allah adalah pasti terlaksana berdasarkan firman-Nya sendiri, bukan karena ada kewajiban eksternal yang mengikat-Nya.

Pembahasan-pembahasan ini mencerminkan kedalaman intelektual tradisi kalam Islam dan relevansinya yang berkelanjutan terhadap pertanyaan-pertanyaan fundamental tentang hakikat Tuhan, kebebasan, dan tanggung jawab moral.
